evidence: ExploreResponseAssembler and SketchResponseAssembler centralize all model-to-JSON translation in one class per handler group, keeping handlers free of serialization concerns.
ExplorationService interface defines a typed contract; ExplorationHandlers depends on the interface, not the SemanticFieldExplorer implementation.
FetchExamplesResult bundles the bcql pattern alongside the results, preventing split-contract reconstruction at the caller.
GrammarConfigSerializer lives in a dedicated class so GrammarConfig and RelationConfig remain pure data carriers with no Jackson dependency.

IOException declared on all four ExplorationService methods leaks raw I/O concerns through a domain-level interface into the HTTP handlers, which re-throw it to wrapWithErrorHandling for generic 500 mapping.
resolveRelationConfig (ExplorationHandlers) embeds a domain-level check (relationType().isEmpty()) at the HTTP parsing layer rather than in the service boundary or domain object.

buildMultiSeedResponseEnvelope accepts String relationType, so handleSemanticFieldComparison passes the CROSS_RELATIONAL sentinel string through the same assembly path as real relation IDs with no type distinction.

SingleSeedExplorationOptions wraps ExplorationOptions with delegation methods (topCollocates, minLogDice, minShared) that simply forward calls to base — the wrapper is useful for type safety but the repeated forwarding adds seam noise.

strengths: All model-to-JSON translation is centralised in dedicated assembler classes (ExploreResponseAssembler, SketchResponseAssembler), so handlers deal only with HTTP parsing and orchestration.
ExplorationService is a clean, typed interface; the HTTP layer depends on the contract, not the implementation, and constructor injection wires dependencies explicitly in WordSketchApiServer.
FetchExamplesResult co-locates the computed BCQL pattern with the results it produced, eliminating the class of bug where callers would re-derive the pattern independently.
GrammarConfig / GrammarConfigLoader / GrammarConfigSerializer are cleanly separated by concern — loading, holding, and serializing are each in their own class with no cross-contamination.
Options type hierarchy (ExplorationOptions / SingleSeedExplorationOptions) makes the distinct call contracts for single-seed vs multi-seed explicit at compile time.
mid_level_elegance.issue_character: The issues are narrow and localized — a raw-IOException leak through the service boundary, and a domain-validation check placed at the HTTP parsing layer — rather than systemic glue-code entropy; each finding represents a single seam that carries slightly more than its layer's responsibility.
mid_level_elegance.score_rationale: The codebase is meaningfully above the 60 'significant drag' threshold: dedicated assemblers, a clean typed interface, and explicit DTOs demonstrate deliberate seam design. What pulls the score below 80 is the IOException leak through the ExplorationService boundary (domain failures reach the HTTP layer as raw IO) and the relationType check embedded in the HTTP parsing helper rather than in the domain boundary where it belongs. These are not structural failures — they are two seams that each carry one concern too many — but they would give a developer navigating the layers a slightly surprising experience.

issues.summary: ExplorationService declares throws IOException, leaking raw I/O concerns through a domain interface into HTTP handlers
evidence: ExplorationService declares 'throws IOException' on exploreByPattern, exploreMultiSeed, compareCollocateProfiles, and fetchExamples.
ExplorationHandlers re-declares 'throws IOException' on each handle method, relying on wrapWithErrorHandling to convert it to a generic HTTP 500.
The HTTP layer cannot distinguish index-not-found from disk-full from network reset — all collapse into the same 500 response path.
A domain interface boundary should not expose java.io.IOException directly; callers at the HTTP layer are forced to handle infrastructure-level failure types.
issues.suggestion: Define a checked domain exception (e.g. ExplorationException wrapping the cause IOException) in the exploration package and declare 'throws ExplorationException' on the interface. ExplorationHandlers can then catch ExplorationException specifically and map it to a structured 500 response. This separates domain failure semantics from raw I/O transport failures and lets wrapWithErrorHandling treat ExplorationException distinctly from unexpected RuntimeExceptions.

issues.summary: ExplorationHandlers.resolveRelationConfig embeds a domain invariant check (relation_type presence) at the HTTP parameter-parsing layer
evidence: resolveRelationConfig in ExplorationHandlers contains: 'if (relationConfig.get().relationType().isEmpty()) { throw new IllegalArgumentException("Invalid relation config: missing or unrecognised relation_type for '" + relationId + "'"); }'
This check enforces a domain invariant (every exploration-capable relation must have a relationType) at the HTTP parsing layer, not in the domain boundary or RelationConfig itself.
Callers bypassing the HTTP layer (e.g. direct SemanticFieldExplorer usage in tests) receive no equivalent validation.

The comment on handleSemanticFieldComparison notes an 'intentional handler asymmetry' because the comparison path bypasses resolveRelationConfig entirely — indicating the validation placement causes callers to reason about bypass paths.
issues.suggestion: Move the relation_type presence check into RelationConfig (a constructor or factory validation) or into the ExplorationService implementation's entry points. A helper method RelationConfig.requireExplorationCapable() that throws IllegalStateException if relationType is absent would enforce the invariant at the domain layer consistently for all callers, removing the seam asymmetry from the HTTP handler.

issues.summary: CROSS_RELATIONAL sentinel is passed as a plain String through the same assembly path as real relation IDs with no type distinction
evidence: CROSS_RELATIONAL = "cross_relational" is a String constant passed to buildMultiSeedResponseEnvelope(String relationType, ...) which also receives real relation IDs from resolveRelationConfig.
buildMultiSeedResponseEnvelope puts the string directly into the parameters map without distinguishing the sentinel case from a real ID.
The asymmetry is documented in the Javadoc of handleSemanticFieldComparison, indicating the caller is aware the seam is not clean.
A consumer reading the parameters map has no way to know the value is a sentinel vs a queryable relation ID without external documentation.
issues.suggestion: Introduce a typed wrapper or use Optional<RelationConfig> as the parameter to the envelope builder, where empty Optional indicates cross-relational mode. This makes the seam self-documenting at the type level. Alternatively, an enum or sealed type distinguishing 'SingleRelation(id)' from 'CrossRelational' would eliminate the sentinel string entirely.

root_causes: The domain layer (ExplorationService) was designed pragmatically to expose checked IOException rather than investing in a domain exception hierarchy, causing raw I/O concerns to surface at the HTTP layer.

Domain validation logic that could live in RelationConfig or the service layer was placed in the HTTP parameter parser for expediency, creating a seam asymmetry between exploration handlers (validated) and comparison handler (bypasses validation).
likely_symptoms: cross_relational_sentinel_string is a symptom of the single-method-for-two-modes envelope builder rather than a root seam design problem.
